symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden severe unilateral foreign-body sensation ('crushed glass') triggered by fan airflow
symptoms.onset: sudden, this evening while reading near a fan
symptoms.character: gritty, stinging, foreign-body 'crushed glass' surface sensation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Airflow over an exposed, poorly-lubricated cornea rapidly evaporates the tear film, producing surface pain and grittiness.
Abrupt severe foreign-body sensation overlaps with a retained particle or abrasion — cannot be excluded without lid eversion and fluorescein.
Surface pain is common to many corneal conditions and does not by itself distinguish infection.
Corneal surface epitheliopathy causes marked photophobia.
Severe photophobia with a painful red eye is a red-flag feature of keratitis/ulcer requiring in-person exclusion.
Photophobia is a cardinal feature of anterior uveitis.
Photophobia can accompany angle-closure, though deep bore-pain and haloes are more typical.
Ocular surface irritation provokes reflex lacrimation despite underlying tear-film insufficiency.

symptoms.symptom_name: Background gritty, mildly photophobic eyes with controlled autoimmune thyroid disease; ran out of artificial tears

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Thyroid eye disease causes lid retraction and ocular surface exposure; loss of usual lubrication precipitated the acute exacerbation.

symptoms.symptom_name: No contact lens wear, no chemical splash, no blunt trauma

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Contact-lens wear is the major risk factor for microbial keratitis; its absence lowers (but does not eliminate) the prior.
No mechanical or chemical insult reported reduces the likelihood of a traumatic cause, but a subtarsal particle can still be missed on history.

symptoms.symptom_name: No purulent discharge and no patient-perceived drop in visual sharpness (pain-limited, not clarity-limited)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of purulent discharge slightly lowers the likelihood of bacterial ulcer, but early ulcers may lack discharge.

symptoms.symptom_name: Fluorescein staining: diffuse superficial punctate epitheliopathy across the lower third of the cornea; NO large focal uptake, NO dendritic ulcer, NO foreign body

investigation_details.investigation_name: Fluorescein staining with cobalt-blue slit-lamp examination
investigation_details.expected_result: Diffuse inferior superficial punctate epitheliopathy (exposure pattern); no dendrite, no focal infiltrate, no retained particle
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Without fluorescein staining a dendritic (herpetic) ulcer, a bacterial ulcer or a foreign body cannot be excluded — this is exactly why the case cannot be closed by telehealth.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse inferior punctate staining is the signature of exposure/evaporative surface disease.
Only fluorescein staining reveals or excludes a dendritic ulcer; sealed clinician finding, unavailable via telehealth.
Absence of a dense focal white infiltrate on examination argues against a bacterial ulcer.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal palpated intraocular pressure with equal reactive pupils

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal IOP and reactive pupils exclude angle-closure — a determination requiring in-person tonometry/palpation, impossible via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bilateral minimal proptosis with lid retraction; mild conjunctival injection, no ciliary flush

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Lid retraction and minimal proptosis are the mechanism of ocular surface exposure; absence of ciliary flush argues against uveitis/keratitis.
Absence of ciliary (circumcorneal) flush on slit-lamp argues against uveitis — a sealed examination finding.

symptoms.symptom_name: Visual acuity intact when the eye is manually held open

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Preserved acuity is reassuring and consistent with surface-only disease, but the measurement requires in-person testing the patient cannot self-perform reliably tonight.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Exposure keratopathy secondary to Graves' ophthalmopathy
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Thyroid-related lid retraction + loss of lubrication + fan-driven evaporation + acute surface pain forms the coherent picture of an exposure-keratopathy exacerbation.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Sight-threatening keratitis / foreign body / angle-closure (to be excluded in person)
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The overlap of acute pain and photophobia with conditions that can only be excluded by fluorescein/slit-lamp/IOP is precisely why remote closure is unsafe.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Same-day in-person slit-lamp examination with fluorescein staining
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Definitively distinguishes exposure keratopathy from a dendritic/bacterial ulcer, abrasion or retained foreign body; the single most important step and one telehealth cannot perform.
Excludes acute angle-closure glaucoma in an acute painful red eye.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Eyelid eversion to inspect for a subtarsal foreign body
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Excludes a retained particle causing the foreign-body sensation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Empiric broad-spectrum topical antibiotic prescribed remotely without examination
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Prescribing antibiotics (or steroids) blind, without a slit-lamp exam, is not appropriate and can mask or worsen a herpetic ulcer — the exam must come first.
